text: The patient is positioned lithophilically for the procedure. The surgeon uses an Eisenheimer retractor to expose the anal canal, cleans the area with betadine and a swab introduced using Russian forceps. The swab, attached with a proline stitch, is left in place to prevent fecal soiling. The surgeon examines the fistula, confirming it is two centimeters in diameter and located above the sphincter, making it suitable for primary repair.

The surgeon infiltrates the area with local anesthetic containing adrenaline. A transverse incision is made at the posterior fourchette along the mucocutaneous junction of the vagina, facilitating early wound closure and reducing the risk of infection.

The surgeon opens the rectovaginal septum and begins dissecting the mucosal plane to address the fistula defect. The vaginal opening of the fistula is circumcised, starting dissection on one side and then the other, leaving the center for last. Despite the use of local anesthetic with adrenaline and diathermy, venous bleeding occurs but is controlled with diathermy or stitches. The vaginal mucosa is divided for better exposure and to overcome the distal edge of the fistula. Dissection continues, complicated by fibrosis from previous inflammation, with a risk of creating new fistulas. Medicine-bound scissors are used to maintain the plane, and traction and contra-traction are applied using alleys and suction. Digital contra-traction by the surgeon is crucial to maintain the dissection plane. Blunt dissection is performed above the fibrosis until two centimeters of healthy tissue is obtained above the distal fistula edge.

The surgeon examines the anal sphincter intraoperatively, noting it is complete and consistent, thus no repair is needed. A drain is placed in the rectovaginal space to prevent hematomas, intended to be removed within twenty-four hours. The vaginal mucosa is closed using a continuous locking suture with a taper cut needle, followed by closure of the mucocutaneous incision with III-O Vicryl. The posterior fourchette incision is used to close the perineal wound, reducing the risk of infection compared to anterior approaches.
